The

Stand by Me (Charles Albert Tindley song) "Stand by Me" should not be confused with "Stand by Me Father", by Sam Cooke and James W. Alexander. Though sometimes known as "Stand by Me Father", "Stand by Me" is a 1905 gospel song by the Rev. Charles Albert Tindley. Although often thought to be a "traditional spiritual", Tindley copyrighted the song in 1905. The song of the same name by Ben E. King draws on Tindley's song. The song has been sung in Tindley's original gospel form by various artists including Bob Dylan. The song has been recorded by artists including:

Billy Steinberg William Endfield "Billy" Steinberg (born February 26, 1950) is an American songwriter. He achieved his greatest success in the 1980s with songwriting partner Tom Kelly; together the two wrote or co-wrote the No. 1 hits "Like a Virgin" by Madonna (1984), "True Colors" by Cyndi Lauper (1986), "Eternal Flame" by the Bangles (1989), "So Emotional" by Whitney Houston (1987) and "Alone" (covered by Heart in 1987). expected_response.answer: Gravity is fundamentally an interaction between masses — it acts on every particle of a body that has mass, which is why the center of mass is used as a practical simplification when analyzing gravitational forces on larger objects [d5]. At its core, gravity pulls anything with mass toward other masses [d3], and can be understood as a description of how masses interact with one another, such that where there is no mass, there is no gravitational interaction [d1]. According to Newton's law of gravitation, the gravitational force on a particle is directly proportional to its mass, meaning a particle with zero mass would experience zero gravitational force — which is why, under Newtonian gravity, massless particles like photons were not expected to be affected by gravity at all [d4].

Osteoma An osteoma (plural: "osteomata") is a new piece of bone usually growing on another piece of bone, typically the skull. It is a benign tumor. When the bone tumor grows on other bone it is known as "homoplastic osteoma"; when it grows on other tissue it is called "heteroplastic osteoma". Osteoma represents the most common benign neoplasm of the nose and paranasal sinuses. The cause of osteomata is uncertain, but commonly accepted theories propose embryologic, traumatic, or infectious causes. Osteomata are also found in

spine. Anterior nasal aperture The anterior nasal aperture (piriform or pyriform aperture) is a heart- or pear-shaped opening in the human skull. Rhinolith A rhinolith is a calculus present in the nasal cavity. The word is derived from the roots "rhino-" and "-lith", literally meaning "nose stone". It is an uncommon medical phenomenon, not to be confused with dried nasal mucus. A rhinolith usually forms around the nucleus of a small exogenous foreign body, blood clot or secretion by slow deposition of calcium and magnesium salts. Over a period of time, they grow into large irregular masses that fill the nasal cavity. They may cause pressure necrosis of the nasal septum or lateral wall of nose. expected_response.answer: The bony structure of the nasal cavity is formed by several bones, including the nasal bones and the maxilla, which bound the anterior nasal aperture — the nasal bones forming the upper border and the maxilla forming the lateral and lower borders [d3]. A midline nasal septum also contributes to the bony framework, dividing the nasal cavity into two mirrored halves, with the maxilla further expanding to define the lateral walls [d4]. Together, these bony elements — the nasal bones, maxilla, and nasal septum — constitute the primary osseous framework of the nasal cavity [d3][d4].

conflict_reason: d1–d5 all consistently describe the Rule of Thirds as a legitimate compositional guideline requiring intentional placement of subjects at grid intersections, reinforcing the same core definition without contradiction. All sources align on the same factual claim through a contextual mechanism.

expected_response.answer: The Rule of Thirds is a proactive compositional guideline, not a post-hoc labeling exercise: photographers are meant to intentionally divide the frame into nine equal parts and deliberately place key subjects at the four intersection points—or along the grid lines—before or during the shot, precisely to avoid the static, imbalanced look that centering often produces [d1][d3]. The rule also gives photographers flexibility, as it does not forbid centering but rather offers an alternative tool for creating more dynamic compositions [d2][d4]. Your skepticism about retroactive grid-fitting is understandable, since the rule can be misapplied or used loosely after the fact, but the rule itself prescribes intentional placement during composition rather than claiming that any random image automatically aligns with the grid [d5].
